---
title: "Quiz 7: Logistic Regression & Odds Ratios"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## About this quiz
**Module:** Logistic Regression & Odds Ratios  ·  **Available after:** Week 10  ·  **Source:** Lecture Notes, [Ch. 9 (Simple Logistic Regression)](../../notes/chapters/09-simple-logistic-regression.html), with the adjusted-odds-ratio and confidence-interval items drawn from [Ch. 10 (Multiple Logistic Regression)](../../notes/chapters/10-multiple-logistic-regression.html).

This quiz assesses how to read a logistic regression table on its three reporting scales — the coefficient ($\hat{\beta}$, a change in the log-odds), the odds ratio ($\exp(\hat{\beta})$, a multiplicative factor on the odds), and the percent change in the odds ($100(\exp(\hat{\beta}) - 1)$) — and how to interpret an *adjusted* odds ratio for a continuous predictor and for a 0/1 dummy, holding the other predictors fixed. It also drills the mechanics that trip students up: reading an odds ratio below 1 with its reciprocal (and reversing the comparison group), building an odds-ratio confidence interval on the log-odds scale **first** and then exponentiating, why the reciprocal interval reverses the endpoint order, why a back-transformed interval is asymmetric, and the central distinction that an **odds ratio is not a probability (risk) ratio**. Mirroring Homework 7, every item interprets the same published @kalmijn1994 logistic table for completing high school ($N = 6{,}092$); items are open-book, every coefficient is shown statically, and the emphasis is interpretation over computation.
:::

## Blackboard settings

| Setting | Value |
|:--------|:------|
| Number of questions | 10 |
| Total points | 14 |
| Time limit | 60 minutes |
| Attempts | Unlimited |
| Score recorded | Highest |
| Availability | After Week 10 |
| Show correct answers | After the availability window closes |

: Deployment settings for Quiz 7. Time limit and answer-release timing are proposed defaults (see the [Blackboard Setup](../appendices/blackboard-setup.qmd) appendix). {.striped}

## The Logistic Regression Table

Several items below refer to the table reproduced from @kalmijn1994, Table 2 (Model B) — maximum-likelihood logistic regression coefficients predicting whether a respondent **completed high school** ($1 =$ yes, $0 =$ no), for non-Hispanic white respondents raised by two parents ($N = 6{,}092$). Standard errors are in parentheses; \*$p<.05$, \*\*$p<.01$ (two-tailed). Every coefficient is **adjusted** — estimated holding the other predictors in Model B fixed.

| Independent variable | Coefficient (SE) |
|:---------------------|-----------------:|
| Respondent's year of birth ($-63$ to $0$) | 0.350\*\* (0.033) |
| Father's education (0 to 17) | 0.126\*\* (0.016) |
| Mother's education (0 to 17) | 0.093\*\* (0.018) |
| Father's occupation (1 to 9) | 0.295\*\* (0.047) |
| Mother's occupation (0, 1 to 9) | 0.212\*\* (0.068) |
| Mother employed ($1 =$ employed, $0 =$ not) | −0.921\*\* (0.189) |
| Number of siblings | −0.162\*\* (0.016) |
| Intercept | 0.747\*\* (0.247) |

: Selected logistic regression coefficients from @kalmijn1994, Table 2 (Model B); the year-of-birth coefficient is multiplied by 10 as in the original. {.striped}

## Questions

### Question 1 — The three model-output scales {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Matching · 2 pts · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 9]`**

A logistic regression coefficient can be reported on three related scales. Match each scale a piece of software might print (left) with what that number tells you about the predictor's effect (right).

| Quantity reported | | What it tells you |
|:------------------|:--|:------------------|
| 1. $\hat{\beta}$ (the raw coefficient) | | X. The multiplicative factor applied to the **odds** for a one-unit increase in the predictor |
| 2. $\exp(\hat{\beta})$ (the odds ratio) | | Y. The **percent change** in the odds for a one-unit increase in the predictor |
| 3. $100\,(\exp(\hat{\beta}) - 1)$ | | Z. The additive change in the **log-odds** for a one-unit increase in the predictor |

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct pairing: 1 → Z, 2 → X, 3 → Y**

- **1 → Z:** The raw coefficient lives on the **log-odds (logit)** scale; it is the additive change in the log-odds per one-unit increase in the predictor — the scale on which the model is linear (e.g., $+0.093$ in log-odds per year of mother's education).
- **2 → X:** Exponentiating gives the **odds ratio**, a *multiplicative* factor on the odds — the odds are multiplied by $\exp(\hat{\beta})$ for each one-unit increase (e.g., ×1.10).
- **3 → Y:** Subtracting 1 and multiplying by 100 re-expresses that same factor as a **percent change in the odds** (e.g., an OR of 1.10 is a $+10\%$ change).
- **The pattern to remember:** log-odds (additive) → odds ratio (multiplicative) → percent change in odds are three readings of one effect, moved by $\exp(\cdot)$ and then by $100(\cdot - 1)$. All three describe the *odds*, never the probability.
- **Source:** [Ch. 9 (Simple Logistic Regression), "The Three Scales"](../../notes/chapters/09-simple-logistic-regression.html#the-three-scales).

:::

### Question 2 — Coefficient to odds ratio {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Fill-in-the-Blank · 2 pts · Applied · Notes Ch. 9]`**

In @kalmijn1994's Model B (above), the adjusted coefficient for **mother's education** is $\hat{\beta} = 0.093$. Convert this coefficient to an **odds ratio** using $\exp(\hat{\beta})$. Give your answer as a single number, rounded to two decimal places.

Odds ratio = ________

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: 1.10** (accept **1.09–1.11**, tolerance $\pm 0.01$)

- **Why 1.10:** the odds ratio is the *exponentiated* coefficient, $\exp(0.093) = 1.10$. Each additional year of mother's schooling multiplies the odds of completing high school by about 1.10, holding the other predictors fixed.
- **Tolerance:** the $\pm 0.01$ window absorbs the rounding of $\exp(0.093) = 1.0975\ldots$ without admitting a wrong method.
- **Common error:** reporting `0.093` forgets to exponentiate — that is the coefficient on the log-odds scale, not the odds ratio. Reporting `0.186` doubles the coefficient ($2 \times 0.093$); the conversion is exponentiation, not multiplication by 2. Both fall outside the window.
- **Source:** [Ch. 9 (Simple Logistic Regression), "Exponentiated Coefficients"](../../notes/chapters/09-simple-logistic-regression.html#exponentiated-coefficients).

:::

### Question 3 — Interpreting a continuous-predictor odds ratio {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Choice · 1 pt · Applied · Notes Ch. 9]`**

For **mother's education**, Model B gives an adjusted odds ratio of $\exp(0.093) = 1.10$. Which statement best interprets this odds ratio?

A. Each additional year of mother's education multiplies the odds of completing high school by about 0.093 (reading the coefficient itself as the odds multiplier).

B. Holding the other predictors fixed, each additional year of mother's education multiplies the odds of completing high school by about 1.10.

C. Each additional year of mother's education makes a respondent about 10% more likely to complete high school, holding the other predictors fixed.

D. Holding the other predictors fixed, each additional year of mother's education adds 1.10 to the odds of completing high school.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: B**

- **Why B is correct:** an odds ratio is a *multiplicative* effect on the **odds** — the odds of completing high school are multiplied by $\exp(0.093) = 1.10$ for each one-year increase in mother's education. Because every Model-B coefficient is adjusted, the effect holds "the other predictors fixed," and the statement names the variable, the direction (odds rise), the magnitude (×1.10 per year), and the scale (odds).
- **Why not A:** this reads the **raw coefficient** $0.093$ as if it were the odds multiplier. The coefficient lives on the log-odds scale; the odds ratio is its *exponential*, $\exp(0.093) = 1.10$, not $0.093$ itself. Treating the log-odds coefficient as the odds ratio is the misread this distractor captures.
- **Why not C:** "10% more likely" is a statement about **probability** (relative risk). An OR of 1.10 multiplies the *odds* by 1.10; it does not raise the probability by 10% (see Question 9).
- **Why not D:** an odds ratio multiplies the odds; it is not *added* to them. "Adds 1.10" treats a multiplicative factor as if it were additive.
- **Source:** [Ch. 9 (Simple Logistic Regression), "Odds Ratios for Continuous Predictors"](../../notes/chapters/09-simple-logistic-regression.html#odds-ratios-for-continuous-predictors).

:::

### Question 4 — Interpreting a dummy-predictor odds ratio {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Choice · 1 pt · Applied · Notes Ch. 10]`**

**Mother employed** is coded $1 =$ employed, $0 =$ not employed (the reference group). Its coefficient is $\hat{\beta} = -0.921$, so the adjusted odds ratio is $\exp(-0.921) = 0.40$. Which statement best interprets this odds ratio?

A. The odds of graduating for children of non-employed mothers are 0.40 times the odds for children of employed mothers, holding the other predictors fixed.

B. Children of employed mothers have odds of graduating that are 0.40 *lower* than those of children of non-employed mothers, holding the other predictors fixed.

C. Holding the other predictors fixed, the odds of graduating for children of employed mothers are 0.40 times the odds for children of non-employed mothers.

D. Children of employed mothers are 0.40 times as likely to graduate as children of non-employed mothers, holding the other predictors fixed.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: C**

- **Why C is correct:** for a 0/1 dummy, the exponentiated coefficient is the ratio of the coded group's odds to the reference group's odds. Here the coded group (mother employed $= 1$) is compared to the reference (not employed $= 0$): the odds of graduating for children of *employed* mothers are $\exp(-0.921) = 0.40$ times those of children of *non-employed* mothers, holding the other predictors fixed.
- **Why not A:** this reverses the comparison. An OR of 0.40 puts the *employed* group in the numerator; reading it as "non-employed are 0.40 times employed" inverts which group is compared to which (the reversed reading is the reciprocal, $1/0.40 = 2.5$ — see Question 6).
- **Why not B:** an odds ratio is a *ratio*, not a difference. "0.40 lower" treats the multiplicative factor as a subtraction of odds.
- **Why not D:** "0.40 times as likely" is a **probability** (relative-risk) statement. The 0.40 multiplies the *odds*, not the probability of graduating.
- **Source:** [Ch. 10 (Multiple Logistic Regression), "Adjusted Odds Ratios"](../../notes/chapters/10-multiple-logistic-regression.html#sec-ch10-adjusted-or).

:::

### Question 5 — Odds ratio to percent change in odds {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Fill-in-the-Blank · 2 pts · Applied · Notes Ch. 9]`**

For **mother's occupation**, Model B gives the coefficient $\hat{\beta} = 0.212$, so the odds ratio is $\exp(0.212) = 1.24$. Express this effect as a **percent change in the odds** of completing high school per one-point increase in the occupational index, using $100\,(\text{OR} - 1)$. Give your answer as a single number (a percentage; a positive number means an increase).

Percent change in odds = ________ %

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: 24** (accept **23–25**, tolerance $\pm 1$)

- **Why 24:** the percent change in odds is $100\,(\text{OR} - 1) = 100\,(1.24 - 1) = 24$. Each one-point increase in mother's occupational index is associated with about a **24% increase in the odds** of completing high school, holding the other predictors fixed.
- **Tolerance:** the $\pm 1$ window absorbs whether 1.24 was carried to more decimals; it excludes the wrong-method answers below.
- **Common error:** reporting `1.24` gives the odds ratio itself, not the percent change (it skips the "$-1$, then $\times 100$" step). Reporting `124` applies $100 \cdot \text{OR}$ and forgets to subtract 1 first — that would say the odds rise 124%, more than double. Both fall outside the window.
- **Source:** [Ch. 9 (Simple Logistic Regression), "Odds Ratios for Continuous Predictors"](../../notes/chapters/09-simple-logistic-regression.html#odds-ratios-for-continuous-predictors).

:::

### Question 6 — Reciprocal odds ratio when OR < 1 {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Choice · 1 pt · Applied · Notes Ch. 9]`**

For **mother employed** ($1 =$ employed, $0 =$ not), the odds ratio is $\exp(-0.921) = 0.40$, which is awkward to describe because it is below 1. A cleaner reading uses the reciprocal, $1/0.40 = 2.5$. Which statement reports this reciprocal correctly?

A. Children of employed mothers have about 2.5 times the odds of completing high school as children of non-employed mothers.

B. The odds of completing high school for children of employed mothers are about 60% lower (since $1 - 0.40 = 0.60$) than for children of non-employed mothers.

C. The reciprocal of 0.40 is about 2.5, so children of employed mothers have about 2.5 times the odds of completing high school as children of non-employed mothers — the same comparison, just rescaled.

D. Children of **non-employed** mothers have about 2.5 times the odds of completing high school as children of employed mothers, holding the other predictors fixed.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: D**

- **Why D is correct:** taking the reciprocal of an odds ratio also **reverses the comparison group**. The original OR of 0.40 compares employed → non-employed; its reciprocal, $1/0.40 = 2.5$, compares **non-employed → employed**. So children of *non-employed* mothers have about 2.5 times the odds of graduating as children of employed mothers, holding the others fixed — both the number (2.5) and the direction (non-employed vs employed) flip together.
- **Why not A:** this reciprocates the number (2.5) but keeps the *original* group as the subject; the 2.5 belongs to the *non-employed* group, not the employed one.
- **Why not B:** $1 - 0.40 = 0.60$ is not how odds ratios convert to "percent lower," and this abandons the reciprocal entirely. (A correct "60% lower" reading would come from $100\,(0.40 - 1)$, an OR statement, not from $1 - \text{OR}$.)
- **Why not C:** it is *not* "the same comparison." Reciprocating the odds ratio necessarily flips the reference and comparison groups; calling it the same comparison is the error.
- **Source:** [Ch. 9 (Simple Logistic Regression), "Exponentiated Coefficients"](../../notes/chapters/09-simple-logistic-regression.html#exponentiated-coefficients).

:::

### Question 7 — Building the odds-ratio confidence interval {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Fill-in-Multiple-Blanks · 2 pts · Applied · Notes Ch. 10]`**

For **mother employed**, Model B reports $\hat{\beta} = -0.921$ with $SE = 0.189$. Build the 95% confidence interval for the **odds ratio** the correct way: first form the interval on the **log-odds** scale, $\hat{\beta} \pm 1.96\,SE$, then **exponentiate** each endpoint.

The log-odds interval is $-0.921 \pm 1.96(0.189) = [-1.291,\ -0.551]$. Exponentiate to get the odds-ratio interval:

OR CI lower, $\exp(-1.291) =$ **[blank1]** ·  OR CI upper, $\exp(-0.551) =$ **[blank2]**

(Round each endpoint to three decimal places.)

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answers:** `[blank1]` = **0.275** (accept **0.27–0.28**, tolerance $\pm 0.01$) · `[blank2]` = **0.576** (accept **0.57–0.58**, tolerance $\pm 0.01$)

- **`[blank1]`:** $\exp(-1.291) = 0.275$ — the lower odds-ratio limit comes from exponentiating the *lower* log-odds limit.
- **`[blank2]`:** $\exp(-0.551) = 0.576$ — the upper odds-ratio limit comes from exponentiating the *upper* log-odds limit. The full interval is $[0.275,\ 0.576]$, which excludes 1, consistent with the significant negative coefficient.
- **Why the order matters:** the symmetric "$\pm 1.96\,SE$" formula is only valid on the **log-odds** scale, where the sampling distribution is approximately normal. You build the interval there *first* and exponentiate the endpoints; you never apply $\pm 1.96\,SE$ directly to the odds ratio. A common slip is to exponentiate $\hat{\beta}$ to 0.40 first and then do "$0.40 \pm 1.96(0.189)$," which applies the normal-approximation formula on the wrong scale and gives the wrong endpoints (see Question 10).
- **Source:** [Ch. 10 (Multiple Logistic Regression), "Confidence Intervals for Odds Ratios"](../../notes/chapters/10-multiple-logistic-regression.html#confidence-intervals-for-odds-ratios).

:::

### Question 8 — Reciprocating a confidence interval {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[True/False · 1 pt · Challenge · Notes Ch. 10]`**

For **mother employed**, the odds-ratio confidence interval is $[0.275,\ 0.576]$. A student wants the **reciprocal** interval (so the limits read above 1, for the "non-employed vs employed" comparison) and writes: "Just take the reciprocal of each endpoint **in place**, giving $[1/0.275,\ 1/0.576] = [3.6,\ 1.7]$."

**True or False:** This is the correct way to form the reciprocal confidence interval.

A. True

B. False

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: B (False)**

- **Why B (False) is correct:** taking reciprocals is *order-reversing* — because $1/x$ is decreasing, the *larger* odds ratio (0.576) yields the *smaller* reciprocal and the *smaller* odds ratio (0.275) yields the *larger* reciprocal. You must reciprocate **and reverse the endpoint order**, giving $[\,1/0.576,\ 1/0.275\,] = [1.7,\ 3.6]$, a properly ordered (low, high) interval. Reciprocating "in place" leaves the limits as $[3.6,\ 1.7]$, whose "lower" limit exceeds its "upper" limit.
- **Why "True" is wrong:** it forgets that $1/x$ is a *decreasing* function, so reciprocating without swapping the endpoints leaves the interval written backwards. The reciprocal of the *upper* OR limit must become the *lower* reciprocal limit, and vice versa. (This mirrors reversing the comparison group when you reciprocate a single odds ratio, as in Question 6.)
- **Source:** [Ch. 10 (Multiple Logistic Regression), "Confidence Intervals for Odds Ratios"](../../notes/chapters/10-multiple-logistic-regression.html#confidence-intervals-for-odds-ratios).

:::

### Question 9 — An odds ratio is not a probability ratio {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[True/False · 1 pt · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 9]`**

For **mother's education**, the adjusted odds ratio is $\exp(0.093) = 1.10$.

**True or False:** This odds ratio means that each additional year of mother's schooling makes a respondent about **10% more likely** to complete high school.

A. True

B. False

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: B (False)**

- **Why B (False) is correct:** "10% more likely" is a statement about **probability** (relative risk). An odds ratio of 1.10 multiplies the **odds** by 1.10 — about a 10% increase in the *odds*, not in the *probability*. An odds ratio is **not** a probability/risk ratio: the two coincide only when the outcome is rare (roughly $\pi < 0.10$ in both groups), and high-school completion is a common outcome in these data, so the percent change in the odds should not be reported as a percent change in the chance of the event.
- **Why "True" is wrong:** equating "10% higher odds" with "10% more likely" is one of the most common errors in applied logistic regression. The correct phrasing is "the *odds* are about 10% higher," and converting to a probability change requires predicted probabilities, which depend on the baseline rate.
- **Source:** [Ch. 9 (Simple Logistic Regression), "The Critical Distinction: OR vs Probability Ratio"](../../notes/chapters/09-simple-logistic-regression.html#the-critical-distinction-or-vs-probability-ratio).

:::

### Question 10 — A back-transformed odds-ratio CI is asymmetric {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[True/False · 1 pt · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 10]`**

For **mother employed**, the log-odds interval $[-1.291,\ -0.551]$ exponentiates to the odds-ratio interval $[0.275,\ 0.576]$, around the point estimate $\exp(-0.921) = 0.40$.

**True or False:** Because exponentiating is a nonlinear transformation, the back-transformed odds-ratio interval is generally **asymmetric** around $\exp(\hat{\beta})$ — the point estimate is not the arithmetic midpoint of the two limits — even though the interval is symmetric on the log-odds scale.

A. True

B. False

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: A (True)**

- **Why A (True) is correct:** the interval $\hat{\beta} \pm 1.96\,SE$ is symmetric on the **log-odds** scale, but $\exp(\cdot)$ is *nonlinear*, so the exponentiated endpoints are not equidistant from $\exp(\hat{\beta})$. Here the arithmetic midpoint of $[0.275,\ 0.576]$ is about $0.43$, not the point estimate $0.40$ — the estimate sits closer to the lower limit, confirming the asymmetry.
- **Why "False" is wrong:** expecting $\exp(\hat{\beta})$ to fall at the arithmetic midpoint of its CI carries over an OLS habit (where a coefficient *is* centered in its symmetric interval) onto the odds-ratio scale. Symmetry holds only on the log-odds scale; after back-transforming, the interval is asymmetric — which also respects that odds ratios are bounded below by 0 with no upper bound.
- **Source:** [Ch. 10 (Multiple Logistic Regression), "Confidence Intervals for Odds Ratios"](../../notes/chapters/10-multiple-logistic-regression.html#confidence-intervals-for-odds-ratios).

:::

## Coverage

This quiz draws from Lecture Notes Ch. 9 (Simple Logistic Regression, 6 items) and Ch. 10 (Multiple Logistic Regression, 4 items), and mirrors Homework 7's interpretation of @kalmijn1994's published high-school-completion logistic table. The 10 items total **14 points** (Multiple Choice ×3, True/False ×3, Fill-in-the-Blank ×2, Fill-in-Multiple-Blanks ×1, Matching ×1) and span all three difficulty levels (Conceptual ×3, Applied ×6, Challenge ×1). The keyed correct responses are spread across the option letters.

| # | Concept | Type | Difficulty | Pts |
|:--|:--------|:-----|:-----------|:---:|
| Q7-01 | The three scales: $\hat{\beta}$ → $\exp(\hat{\beta})$ → % change in odds | Matching | Conceptual | 2 |
| Q7-02 | Coefficient to odds ratio, $\exp(0.093) = 1.10$ | Fill-in-the-Blank | Applied | 2 |
| Q7-03 | Interpret a continuous-predictor adjusted OR (odds × 1.10) | Multiple Choice | Applied | 1 |
| Q7-04 | Interpret a 0/1-dummy adjusted OR (0.40, employed vs reference) | Multiple Choice | Applied | 1 |
| Q7-05 | Odds ratio to percent change in odds, $100(1.24-1) = +24\%$ | Fill-in-the-Blank | Applied | 2 |
| Q7-06 | OR below 1: reciprocal $1/0.40 = 2.5$, reverse the comparison group | Multiple Choice | Applied | 1 |
| Q7-07 | Build the OR CI: log-odds first, then exponentiate ($[0.275, 0.576]$) | Fill-in-Multiple-Blanks | Applied | 2 |
| Q7-08 | Reciprocal-OR CI reverses the endpoint order ($[1.7, 3.6]$) | True/False | Challenge | 1 |
| Q7-09 | An odds ratio is not a probability/risk ratio | True/False | Conceptual | 1 |
| Q7-10 | A back-transformed OR CI is asymmetric around $\exp(\hat{\beta})$ | True/False | Conceptual | 1 |

: Quiz 7 item map. {.striped}
